request.question: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?

title: Eloise Jarvis McGraw
Mark Jonathan Harris
Eastern Shore League
Bobby Unser
Tim Duncan
Graeme Hick
John Salley
Gene Upshaw
Herb Thomas (baseball)
Duran Duran discography
sentences: Eloise Jarvis McGraw (December 9, 1915 – November 30, 2000) was an American author of children's books and young adult novels.
She was awarded the Newbery Honor three times in three different decades, for her novels "Moccasin Trail" (1952), "The Golden Goblet" (1962), and "The Moorchild" (1997). "
A Really Weird Summer" (1977) won an Edgar Award for Best Juvenile Mystery from the Mystery Writers of America.
McGraw had a very strong interest in history, and among the many books she wrote for children are "Greensleeves", "The Seventeenth Swap", and "Mara, Daughter of the Nile".
A Lewis Carroll Shelf Award was given to "Moccasin Trail" in 1963.
Mark Jonathan Harris (born 1941) is an American documentary filmmaker probably best known for his films "" (2000) and "The Long Way Home" (1997).
He has directed three documentaries which have gone on to win Oscars, across three different decades.
The Eastern Shore Baseball League was a class D minor league baseball league that operated on the Delmarva Peninsula for parts of three different decades.
The league's first season was in 1922 and the last was in 1949, although the years were not consecutive, and featured teams from Maryland, Delaware, and Virginia.
The first incarnation lasted from 1922 to mid-1928 (disbanded in July), the second from 1937–41, and the third from 1946–49.
Though the level of play was competitive and many future major leaguers gained experience in the ESBL, funding the league remained a constant problem for the rural franchises.
Robert William "Bobby" Unser (born February 20, 1934) is an American former automobile racer.
He is the brother of Al Unser, Jerry Unser and Louis Unser, the father of Robby Unser, and the uncle of Al Unser, Jr. and Johnny Unser.
He is one of ten drivers to win the Indianapolis 500 three or more times, and one of only two (followed by Rick Mears) to have won the 500 in three different decades (1968, 75, 81).
Bobby has also been a spokesman and advocate of many commercial products.

Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.
He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.
Graeme Ashley Hick MBE (born 23 May 1966) is a former English cricketer who played 65 Test matches and 120 One Day Internationals for England.

He played county cricket for Worcestershire for his entire English domestic career, a period of well over twenty years, and in 2008 he surpassed Graham Gooch's record for the most matches in all forms of the game combined.

He scored more than 40,000 first-class runs, mostly from number three in the order, and he is one of only three players to have passed 20,000 runs in List A cricket (Graham Gooch and Sachin Tendulkar are the others) and is one of only twenty-five players to have scored 100 centuries in first-class cricket.
He is the only cricketer who scored first-class triple hundreds in three different decades (1988, 1997 and 2002).
He is the second highest run scorer of all time after Graham Gooch.

Despite these achievements, he is commonly held to have underachieved in international cricket, a view based on comparison of Hick's overall first-class batting average of 52.23 "vis-à-vis" his Test average of 31.32.
John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.

He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

Eugene Thurman Upshaw Jr. (August 15, 1945 – August 20, 2008), also known as "Uptown Gene", was an American football player for the Oakland Raiders of the American Football League (AFL) and later the National Football League (NFL).
He later served as the executive director of the National Football League Players' Association (NFLPA).
In 1987, he was inducted into the Pro Football Hall of Fame.
He is also the only player in NFL history to reach the Super Bowl in three different decades with the same team.
Herbert Mark Thomas (May 26, 1902 in Sampson City, Florida – December 4, 1991 in Starke, Florida) was a right-handed minor and major league baseball player and minor league manager.
Although his major league career lasted only three seasons, 1924, 1925 and 1927, for two different teams, the Boston Braves and New York Giants, his minor league career spanned 17 seasons.
He played professionally in three different decades.

British band Duran Duran have released fourteen studio albums, three remix albums, two live albums, four compilation albums, two box sets, five EPs, thirty-nine singles, fourteen video albums on various record labels. Duran Duran have sold over 100 million records.

The band is one of the few artists to achieve US top 10 albums in three different decades (1980s, 1990s and 2010s) and one of even fewer to reach the top 20 in all four decades of their existence: five albums in the 1980s ("Duran Duran", "Rio", "Seven and the Ragged Tiger", "Arena" and "Notorious"), three albums in the 1990s ("Liberty", "Duran Duran" [aka "The Wedding Album"] and "Thank You"), one album in the 2000s ("Astronaut") and one album in the 2010s ("Paper Gods").

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.hop1_query: For Which team did the second man to win a championship in three different decades play ?
hop1_docs: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005 | The Indian cricket team toured Zimbabwe for cricket matches in August and September 2005.  The Indians played in the Videocon Tri-Series in Zimbabwe, winning three of five matches but losing the final to finish second in the three-team tournament, and they played Zimbabwe for two Test matches in September 2005.  The hosts Zimbabwe were ninth out of ten teams in the ICC Test Championship, with their last win against the top eight Test nations coming in June 2001, against India.  They did not manage to win a match here either, as India, led by the performances of fast bowler Irfan Pathan (who took 21 wickets), won the series 2–0 after an innings win in the first Test and a 10-wicket win in the second.  India consolidated their third place in the Test championship with the win, which was also their first series win in Zimbabwe in four attempts.

John Salley | John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964) is an American retired professional basketball player, actor and talk show host.  He was the first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises, as well as the first player (and only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan) in the NBA to win a championship in three different decades.

2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships | 2008 Dutch Caribbean Age Group Championships is a championship for young Track & Field athletes of the islands of the Dutch Kingdom.  In 2008 there were three island competing.  Aruba, Curaçao and Sint Maarten.  They compete in different events and the athlete with the overall most points will be declared the winner.  There are two age groups, they are 10–11 years (Pentathlon) and 12–13 years (Heptathlon).  This year was the first year that hurdles and baseball throw was added to the event list for the heptathlon.  Sint Eustatius did not have a complete team.  They only competed with a full Heptathlon team.  Like every year, the hosting island is allowed to have two teams (which was Aruba).  There were 32 athletes competing in total and Curaçao was declared the overall winner, Aruba came in second and Sint Maarten came in third.  This would also mark the 4th win for Curaçao

1894–95 British Home Championship | The 1894-95 British Home Championship was an international football tournament played between the British Home Nations.  The competition was won by England, who like second placed Wales, did not lose a game.  Wales however failed to win one either, scoring three draws and so finishing behind England.  Scotland took joint second place with three points gained from a win, draw and a loss.  Ireland came last with a single point garnered from their draw with Wales.

1920–21 British Home Championship | The 1920-21 British Home Championship was a football tournament played between the British Home Nations during the 1920–21 season.  The second tournament played since the hiatus of the First World War, the 1921 competition was dominated by Scotland, who won the first of seven championships they would claim throughout the decade.  England and reigning champions Wales came joint second as goal difference was not at this stage used to separate teams.

1978–79 British Home Championship | The 1978-79 British Home Championship was a British Home Nations competition, won by the English football side and notable for seeing marked increases in hooliganism and falling attendance which would result in its cancellation in 1984.  The English started well, beating Northern Ireland to match the heavy Welsh victory over Scotland on the same day, which featured a hat trick by John Toshack.  Scotland recovered by beating the Irish in their next match while England and Wales played out a goalless draw, leaving three sides theoretically capable of winning the Championship in the final round.  Wales could only manage a draw with the Irish and so in the deciding match between England and Scotland, a 1–1 half time score gave the Scots some hope but a strong second half performance from England was rewarded with a deserved 3–1 win.  This result gave England the Championship, with Wales in second place.  The tournament also saw the introduction of goal difference to separate teams, although it had no effect on the eventual outcome.

1955–56 British Home Championship | The 1955-56 British Home Championship was a football tournament played during the 1955–56 season between the British Home Nations.  It was the only occasion during the hundred-year run of the Home Championship in which all four teams finished level on points.  As goal difference was not used to determine position until 1979, all four teams shared the trophy, holding it for three months each.  Had goal difference been used to determine the winner, then England would have won with Scotland second.
hop1_titles: Indian cricket team in Zimbabwe in 2005
response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found:
  - "John Thomas Salley (born May 16, 1964)" and "first player in NBA history to play on three different championship-winning franchises" — passage [2].
  - "only one of two, the other being Tim Duncan" (explicitly naming Tim Duncan as the second person) — passage [2].
- Ambiguities:
  - Two candidate people are mentioned: "John Thomas Salley" and "Tim Duncan" (as written in passage [2]). The passages do NOT contain Tim Duncan's team(s) or a Tim Duncan page; thus we cannot assume which candidate the question intends if multiple interpretations exist.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact:
  - Bridge entity (as named in the passages): "Tim Duncan".
  - Missing precise fact needed: the NBA team (franchise name) that Tim Duncan played for (e.g., the primary team listed on his page/infobox).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open the "Tim Duncan" page — check the lede and infobox and the "NBA career"/"Career" or "Teams" section for which team(s) he played for. Search keywords: "Tim Duncan played for", "Tim Duncan team", "Tim Duncan infobox".
  2) If needed for disambiguation, open the "John Salley" page — check lede/infobox and "Career" or "Teams" to confirm his ordering/status. Search keywords: "John Salley teams", "John Thomas Salley teams".
  3) If Tim Duncan page is ambiguous, fetch "Tim Duncan (disambiguation)" or check NBA career history sections (season-by-season) on his page.
Final answer deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: Tim Duncan (American football) | Tim Duncan (born June 12, 1979) is a former American football placekicker who played in the National Football League (NFL) for the Arizona Cardinals.  He played college football at the University of Oklahoma.  Duncan also played for Kentucky State University in the division II SIAC conference during the 1997 season when he helped the Thoroughbreds win the inaugural Pioneer Bowl.  He transferred to Oklahoma the following season.

Tricia Duncan | Patricia "Tricia" Duncan (born August 21, 1973) is a retired swimmer for the U.S. Virgin Islands who participated in the backstroke at the 1988 Summer Olympics.  Duncan finished 34th in the 100 m backstroke and 30th in 200 m backstroke.  She is the older sister of NBA basketball player Tim Duncan.  Duncan attended Swarthmore College.

Ray Duncan | Raymond Twomey "Ray" Duncan (October 23, 1930 – October 9, 2015) was an American entrepreneur and vintner.  Originally involved in oil entrepreneurship in Colorado and founder of Duncan Oil, he was the founder along with Justin Meyer of Silver Oak Cellars in 1972, a successful winery based in the Napa Valley and Alexander Valley.  Today his sons David Duncan and Tim Duncan run Silver Oak Cellars, as well as Twomey Cellars.  Duncan established the Durango Mountain "Purgatory" Ski Resort in 1965 and was chairman of the board for the ski industry organization Colorado Ski Country USA.  He was inducted into the Colorado Ski and Snowboard Hall of Fame in 2006.

IBM Award | The IBM Award was an award given out to National Basketball Association players from 1984 to 2002.  The award was sponsored and calculated by technology company IBM and was determined by a computer formula, which measured a player's statistical contribution to his team.  The player with the best contribution to his team in the league received the award.  The first recipient was Magic Johnson of the Los Angeles Lakers, and the final recipient was Tim Duncan of the San Antonio Spurs.

David Duncan (vintner) | David Duncan (born 1965) is an American vintner and entrepreneur.  The President and Chief Executive Officer of Duncan Oil, he is also the President and Chief Executive Officer of Silver Oak Cellars and Managing Partner of Twomey Cellars, successful wineries in northern California, operating in the Napa, Alexander and Russian River Valleys.  He is the son of late Ray Duncan, an oil and wine entrepreneur who co-founded Silver Oak in 1972 with Justin Meyer.  Silver Oak is a family-run and owned business, and his brother Tim Duncan is Executive Vice President of the company. " ColoradoBiz" says that David Duncan is "as much a hands-on winery president as he can be", as president of a winery that has "reached cult status" for its Cabernet Sauvignon production.

Silver Oak Cellars | Silver Oak Cellars is a family-owned, California winery dedicated to producing only Cabernet Sauvignon.  Silver Oak was established in 1972 by Ray Duncan and Justin Meyer.  It is currently operated by Ray's sons David Duncan, who serves as the President and CEO, and his brother, Tim Duncan, who serves as Executive Vice President.  Silver Oak has two wineries; one in Oakville in the Napa Valley and one in Geyserville in the Alexander Valley.  The Duncan Family also established Twomey Cellars in 1999, named after Ray Duncan’s mother, where they produce Pinot noir, Merlot and Sauvignon blanc.  Twomey has wineries in Calistoga and Healdsburg.

Tim Duncan | Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player who played his entire 19-year career with the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).  Widely considered to be the greatest power forward of all time, he is a five-time NBA champion, two-time NBA MVP, three-time NBA Finals MVP, NBA All-Star Game MVP and NBA Rookie of the Year.  He is also a 15-time NBA All-Star and the only player to be selected to both the All-NBA and All-Defensive Teams in all of his first 13 seasons.
response.summary_2: Tim Duncan played for the San Antonio Spurs. Timothy Theodore Duncan (born April 25, 1976) is an American retired professional basketball player.
